preFix.title: Disposing of electronics responsibly
preFix.subtitle: WEEE regulations, council collection, and retailer take-back schemes
preFix.excerpt: Electronic waste is the fastest-growing waste stream in the UK and contains materials that are toxic in landfill and valuable if recovered. In England you cannot legally put electronics in household rubbish bins. The alternatives are free and straightforward.
preFix.sourceNotes: WRAP WEEE compliance guidance for consumers. Environment Agency WEEE regulations enforcement notes. Which? retailer take-back scheme audit (2024). Recycle Now WEEE finder tool documentation.

content.text: The WEEE category covers everything from mobile phones and laptops to kitchen appliances, power tools, electric toothbrushes. And battery-powered toys. UK householders generated about 1.5 million tonnes of WEEE in 2023. Of which roughly 40 percent was collected and recycled via compliant routes. The remainder entered general waste streams.

All HWRCs in England accept WEEE free of charge. Most have a dedicated WEEE area. Large appliances are typically accepted in a separate bay. HWRC opening hours and accepted items vary by council. Check the local authority website before travelling with large items.

Under WEEE rules. Any retailer that sells electrical goods must either take back an old item on delivery or run an in-store drop-off point. This applies to online shops as well as physical stores. Currys, Argos, John Lewis, and most supermarkets have collection points for phones, batteries, and cables.

Many electronics manufacturers run take-back schemes. Apple, Samsung, Dell, and HP all offer collection or trade-in. Even devices with no trade-in value can usually be returned to the manufacturer for recycling.

Working electronics should be offered for reuse before recycling. Community Repaint and Computer Aid International collect working devices. Charity shops accept working small appliances. The Restart Project runs repair cafes where volunteers help fix broken electronics. A device that is repaired or reused rather than recycled retains its embedded material value and delays the energy cost of manufacturing a replacement.

Batteries are a sub-category of WEEE. All retailers that sell batteries must provide a collection point. A small box next to the battery display in any supermarket is the standard collection point. Remove batteries from devices before dropping the device at a HWRC: batteries embedded in equipment are harder to recover during processing.

Before donating or recycling any device that has held personal data. Do a factory reset or use disk-wiping software. For hard drives being recycled, drilling through the platter ensures data cannot be recovered. Unlink any cloud-connected device from its accounts before disposal.
